The people’s sin was that they complained. See how sinful sin is, taking advantage of the commandment to provoke anger. The law revealed sin, but could not destroy it; it restrained, but could not conquer. They complained. Those who are discontented will always find something to complain or worry about, even if their circumstances are very favorable. The Lord heard them, though Moses did not. God knows the secret anxieties and murmurs of the heart, even when they are hidden from others. What he noticed displeased him greatly, and he punished them for this sin. Their anger toward God burned in their minds; rightly, God’s wrath fell upon their bodies. But God's judgments came gradually so that they might take warning. It appeared that God does not delight in punishing; when he begins, he is quickly persuaded to stop.

Man, having abandoned his proper rest, feels uneasy and wretched even when prosperous. They were tired of the food God had provided for them, though it was wholesome and nourishing. It cost no money or effort, and gathering it required very little labor; yet they spoke of Egypt’s cheapness and the fish they ate freely there, as if that cost them nothing, when they paid dearly for it with hard service! While they ate manna, they seemed exempt from the curse sin has brought on mankind—that people must eat bread in the sweat of their faces—yet they spoke of it with scorn. Grumbling, discontented minds will find fault with something even if there is nothing wrong with it, simply because it is too good for them. Those who could be happy often make themselves miserable through discontent. They could not be satisfied unless they had meat to eat. It shows the power of the carnal mind when we desire the pleasures and satisfaction of the senses. We should not indulge in any desire that we cannot turn into prayer, as we cannot when we ask for food to satisfy our lusts. What is lawful in itself becomes evil when God does not allot it to us, yet we desire it.

The provocation was great; yet Moses expressed himself inappropriately. He undervalued the honor God had placed upon him. He magnified his own accomplishments while he had Divine wisdom to guide him and Almighty power to dispense rewards and punishments. He spoke distrustfully of God’s grace. Had the work been much less, he could not have accomplished it in his own strength; but if it had been much greater, through God strengthening him, he might have done it. Let us pray, Lord, lead us not into temptation.

Moses is to choose elders—that is, wise and experienced men. God promises to qualify them. If they were not found fit for the task, they should be made fit. Even the discontented people will be satisfied so that every mouth may be stopped. Here we see I. The emptiness of all sensory pleasures; they will cloy but will not satisfy. Only spiritual pleasures will satisfy and last. As the world passes away, so do its desires. 2. What brutish sins gluttony and drunkenness are! They harm the body instead of nourishing it. Moses objects. Even true believers sometimes find it difficult to trust God under the pressures of secondary causes, and against hope to believe in hope. God here brings Moses to this point: The Lord God is Almighty; and puts the proof on the outcome: You will see whether my word will come to pass or not. If he speaks, it is done.

Here is the fulfillment of God’s promise to Moses that he would have help in governing Israel. He gave of his Spirit to seventy elders. They spoke to the people about spiritual things so that all who heard them might say that God was with them indeed. Two of the elders, Eldad and Medad, did not go out to the tabernacle with the others, being aware of their own weakness and unworthiness. But the Spirit of God found them in the camp, and there they exercised their gift of praying, preaching, and praising God; they spoke as moved by the Holy Ghost. The Spirit of God is not confined to the tabernacle but, like the wind, blows where it pleases. Those who humble themselves will be exalted; those who are most fit for government desire it least. Joshua did not want them punished, but only restrained from speaking in the future. He made this request out of zeal for what he thought was the unity of the church. He wanted them silenced so they would not cause a division or rival Moses; but Moses was not afraid of such effects coming from the Spirit God had placed upon them. Should we reject those whom Christ has approved, or restrain anyone from doing good because they are not in every way like us? Moses wished that all the Lord’s people were prophets so that he would put his Spirit on all of them. Let the testimony of Moses be believed by those who desire to be in power: government is a burden. It is a burden of care and trouble to those who take their duty seriously; and to those who do not, it will prove an even heavier burden on the day of account. Let the example of Moses be followed by those in power: let them not despise the advice and assistance of others but desire it and be thankful for it. If all the present number of the Lord’s people were made prophets or ministers by the Spirit of Christ, though they did not agree on outward matters, there is enough work for everyone in calling sinners to repentance and faith in our Lord Jesus.

God fulfilled his promise to the people by giving them meat. How much more diligent men are in collecting food that perishes than in laboring for food that endures to everlasting life! We are quick to understand worldly matters, but stupidity blinds us regarding eternal concerns. To pursue worldly advantages, we need no arguments; but when it comes to securing true riches, then we are all forgetful. Those who are under the power of a carnal mind will have their desires fulfilled, even though it leads to the certain damage and ruin of their precious souls. They paid dearly for their feasts. God often grants the desires of sinners in anger while he denies the desires of his own people in love. What we unduly desire, if we obtain it, we should fear will be a grief or trial to us. And how many there are everywhere who shorten their lives by overindulgence of one kind or another! Let us seek those pleasures that satisfy but never cloy; and which will endure forevermore.
